topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias slammed his laptop shut, the sharp click echoing in the otherwise silent studio. He leaned back in his worn leather chair, running a hand through his already disheveled hair. The melody, his intricate arrangement, had been butchered. He’d been so meticulous in the demo, every subtle chord change calculated, yet the version he’d just heard—a raw, clumsy rendition by some stranger—sounded like a cruel mockery. He picked up his guitar, the wood feeling cold and alien beneath his fingertips. He started playing the piece again, louder this time, trying to force the memory of his perfect execution back into his mind, but the phantom sound of the other musician’s interpretation kept bleeding into his thoughts, twisting his focus into a knot of tight tension in his chest.

[story 2]

"No," she muttered, her voice tight enough to snap. Sarah paced the small dressing room, the worn carpet muffling her hurried steps. The recording had been sent out, and the feedback she received was… underwhelming. She stopped abruptly, staring at the blinking cursor on her monitor. It was the track she’d poured six months of sleepless nights into. Then, the notification popped up: a live performance clip. As the opening notes of her song filled the room, a familiar, yet utterly wrong, timbre washed over her. The way the vocalist bent the high notes, the way the rhythm section lagged just slightly—it felt like someone had taken her soul and rearranged the furniture. Sarah gripped the edge of the vanity, her knuckles white against the cool glass. She wanted to scream at the air, to demand that the world listen to *her* version, not this sloppy echo.

[story 3]

Marcus stared blankly at the playback screen. His jaw was clenched so tightly he could feel the strain in his molars. He’d spent weeks refining that bridge, the emotional core of the entire composition, and now it was being spun by someone who clearly didn't grasp the nuance. He stood up, walking to the window and staring out at the indifferent city lights, the vastness amplifying his sense of inadequacy. When the recording began, the familiar opening riff hit, but the subsequent vocal delivery was jarringly off-key, a clumsy approximation that stripped the song of all its intended weight. Marcus clenched his fists at his sides, the energy vibrating through his arms. He shook his head slowly, a deep, weary sigh escaping his lips. *They don't get it,* he thought, the silence of the room suddenly heavy with his own self-reproach.
